prediction: CacheLib's hot/cold tiering works by assigning a single binary bit (hot or cold) to each cache item, keeping hot items in fast DRAM and cold items in slower SSD/remote storage. Items promote (cold→hot on access) or demote (hot→cold when idle). This binary simplicity—one bit of metadata per item, no counters or rankings—delivers most of the benefit of elaborate schemes like LFU (access counters) or LIRS (inter-reference recency values) without their overhead. The CRASH hypothesis states that numerical bookkeeping (maintaining integer counts or continuous IRR values) causes LLM reasoning failures, while binary categorical bookkeeping (hot/cold labels) is tractable because it requires no arithmetic. For LLM variable management: label variables HOT if you'll use them in the next few iterations, COLD if you've moved past them. Under token pressure, evict cold variables first. This mirrors exactly why CacheLib powers 70+ Facebook production systems—the one-bit abstraction is simple enough to reason about correctly while still capturing the dominant signal about what to keep vs. evict.
